canonical.summary: Lesions first develop on lower leaves as small, brownish-black spots which can expand to about 1/4-1/2 inch in diameter with characteristic concentric rings (bulls-eye pattern) in the darkened area, usually surrounded by a yellow halo; lesions coalesce and can kill entire leaves; the disease can also move to stems and fruits, producing leathery, sunken, dark lesions with concentric rings near the calyx/stem end of fruit.
diagnostic_features: Lesions display a distinctive "bulls-eye" pattern of concentric rings (visible with a hand lens), usually surrounded by a yellow halo, appearing first on older/lower leaves; fruit lesions are sunken, leathery, and dark, often near the calyx end with concentric ridges.

summary.value: Lesions first develop on lower leaves as small, brownish-black spots which can expand to about 1/4-1/2 inch in diameter with characteristic concentric rings (bulls-eye pattern) in the darkened area, usually surrounded by a yellow halo; lesions coalesce and can kill entire leaves; the disease can also move to stems and fruits, producing leathery, sunken, dark lesions with concentric rings near the calyx/stem end of fruit.

diagnostic_features.quote: A yellow halo usually surrounds the lesions. The lesions develop a "bulls-eye" pattern of concentric rings that can be seen with a hand lens.

canonical.summary: Southern blight initially appears as water-soaked lesions on lower leaves or stems, leading to yellowing, wilting, and plant death, often with white fungal mats and small tan to dark reddish-brown sclerotia on infected tissue and surrounding soil.
diagnostic_features: White mycelium and tan to reddish-brown, spherical sclerotia (1 to 2 mm in diameter) on stem lesions; fruit lesions become water-soaked, soft, and star-shaped with no offensive odor.

summary.value: Southern blight initially appears as water-soaked lesions on lower leaves or stems, leading to yellowing, wilting, and plant death, often with white fungal mats and small tan to dark reddish-brown sclerotia on infected tissue and surrounding soil.
summary.quote: Southern blight initially leads to a water-soaked appearance on lower leaves or water-soaked lesions (spots) on lower stems.  Any plant part that is near or in contact with the soil may become infected.  Infected plants yellow and wilt, often within days of infection... Thick mats of white fungal threads (called mycelia) may grow from infected tissue, radiating from the plant onto the soil surface.  Sclerotia (small spherical structures that are about the size of mustard seeds) develop on infected tissue and on the soil surface.

diagnostic_features.value: White mycelium and tan to reddish-brown, spherical sclerotia (1 to 2 mm in diameter) on stem lesions; fruit lesions become water-soaked, soft, and star-shaped with no offensive odor.
diagnostic_features.quote: tan to reddish-brown, spherical sclerotia (1 to 2 mm in diameter) can appear on the mat of mycelia... pathogen_scientific_name.quote: Southern blight is caused by the soil-born fungus Agroathelia rolfsii (= Sclerotium rolfsii ).

type_of_disease.quote: Southern blight is a lethal fungal disease that is most common in the tropics and subtropics.

canonical.summary: Seedling infection causes pre- or post-emergence damping-off; yellowing and wilting are the first symptoms observed in the field, followed by light brown lesions on stems near the soil surface that later darken into a dark brown girdling lesion at the stem base, with leaves turning brown and often remaining attached, accompanied by a conspicuous white fan-like mat of mycelium and tan to brown sclerotia on infected lower stems.
diagnostic_features: Conspicuous white, fan-like mats of fungal mycelium on the base of the stem, leaf residue, and surrounding soil, plus numerous small round sclerotia about the size of mustard seeds that progress from yellow-tan to reddish-brown to dark brown at maturity.

summary.value: Seedling infection causes pre- or post-emergence damping-off; yellowing and wilting are the first symptoms observed in the field, followed by light brown lesions on stems near the soil surface that later darken into a dark brown girdling lesion at the stem base, with leaves turning brown and often remaining attached, accompanied by a conspicuous white fan-like mat of mycelium and tan to brown sclerotia on infected lower stems.
summary.quote: Seedling infection results in pre- or post-emergence damping-off. Later in the season, entire plants may become yellow and wilt, with leaves turning brown and often remaining attached to the plant. A dark brown lesion that girdles the stem occurs at the soil surface. This lesion is generally accompanied by the development of conspicuous white, fanlike mats of fungal mycelium that form on the base of the stem, on leaf residue, and on the soil surface around infected plants.

diagnostic_features.value: Conspicuous white, fan-like mats of fungal mycelium on the base of the stem, leaf residue, and surrounding soil, plus numerous small round sclerotia about the size of mustard seeds that progress from yellow-tan to reddish-brown to dark brown at maturity.
diagnostic_features.quote: This lesion is generally accompanied by the development of conspicuous white, fanlike mats of fungal mycelium that form on the base of the stem, on leaf residue, and on the soil surface around infected plants. Numerous, small round fungal bodies that are about the size of mustard seeds (called sclerotia) form on these fungal mats and on the lower stem. Initially, sclerotia are yellow-tan then progress to a reddish-brown color and finally dark brown at maturity.

affected_parts.quote: Yellowing and wilting are usually the first symptoms observed ( Figure 1 ). Additionally, light brown lesions develop on stems near soil surface and later darken ( Figure 2 ). ... Once sclerotia germinate, hyphae (fungal roots) invade plant roots or lower stems.

canonical.summary: Light purple spots develop on the top surface of leaves exposed to light, expanding and deepening to reddish-purple or bronze, with infected leaves appearing leathery and 'sunburned'.
diagnostic_features: Leathery, 'sunburned' appearance of infected leaves with reddish-purple or bronze discoloration on the upper surface; red-brown spots may develop on both leaf surfaces and coalesce into large necrotic areas leading to upper canopy defoliation; reddish-purple lesions on petioles, stems, and pods. Discoloration is caused by the light-dependent toxin cercosporin produced by C. kikuchii.

profiles.disease: Bacterial Canker And Wilt Of Tomato

canonical.summary: Initial symptoms include leaf curling, wilting, chlorosis, and shriveling of lower leaves, progressing to wilt, yellowing, browning, and stem splitting with cankers; characteristic 'bird's eye spots' (white to yellow spots, 3-4 mm with raised brown centers) appear on green fruit.
diagnostic_features: Characteristic 'bird's eye spots' on green fruit (white to yellow spots, 3-4 mm with raised brown centers); one-sided wilting of leaves; downward cupping of leaves; marginal scorch or 'firing' of leaves; stems split open revealing internal yellowish to dark reddish brown discoloration of vascular tissue; small white blister-like spots surrounded by dark rings on leaves of young greenhouse plants.

summary.value: Initial symptoms include leaf curling, wilting, chlorosis, and shriveling of lower leaves, progressing to wilt, yellowing, browning, and stem splitting with cankers; characteristic 'bird's eye spots' (white to yellow spots, 3-4 mm with raised brown centers) appear on green fruit.
summary.quote: Initial symptoms are the result of primary, systemic infection and first affect the lower leaves causing leaf curling, wilting, chlorosis, and shriveling... Spots occur on green fruit and are very characteristic: white to yellow spots, 3-4 mm with raised brown centers (“bird’s eye spots”).

diagnostic_features.value: Characteristic 'bird's eye spots' on green fruit (white to yellow spots, 3-4 mm with raised brown centers); one-sided wilting of leaves; downward cupping of leaves; marginal scorch or 'firing' of leaves; stems split open revealing internal yellowish to dark reddish brown discoloration of vascular tissue; small white blister-like spots surrounded by dark rings on leaves of young greenhouse plants.
diagnostic_features.quote: Spots occur on green fruit and are very characteristic: white to yellow spots, 3-4 mm with raised brown centers (“bird’s eye spots”)... Early symptoms include one-sided wilting of leaves, and downward cupping of leaves. Later, stems may develop adventitious roots, external brown streaks, or may split open revealing internal discoloration of vascular tissue... small, white, blister-like spots surrounded by dark rings form on leaves of young plants.

affected_parts.value: Foliar; Stem; Vascular; Pod; Whole plant
affected_parts.quote: Initially, symptoms of bacterial canker on leaves appear on the leaflet margins... The bacterium can become systemic (occurring throughout the plant) and this results in wilting of the plant. Stems and pith can develop streaks of discoloration and the pith becomes mealy. Lesions on fruit will be raised, dark-colored spots with a light yellow or cream-colored halo.

pathogen_scientific_name.quote: The disease is caused by the bacterial pathogen Clavibacter michiganensis subspecies michiganensis .

type_of_disease.quote: Clavibacter michiganensis is an aerobic, non-motile, non-spore forming, non-acid-fast, unencapsulated, gram-positive bacteria.

canonical.summary: Green to yellow mottling (blotchiness) of younger leaves in the upper canopy, with possible leaf puckering, distortion, and plant stunting. Pods can be misshapen and mottled, and seeds may show smoky discoloration or seed coat mottling.
diagnostic_features: Green stems remaining after pods have matured, along with green-to-yellow mottling on younger upper-canopy leaves and seed coat mottling (brown or black depending on hilum color); rugosity (distortion or wrinkling) combined with chlorotic mottling on foliage.

summary.value: Green to yellow mottling (blotchiness) of younger leaves in the upper canopy, with possible leaf puckering, distortion, and plant stunting. Pods can be misshapen and mottled, and seeds may show smoky discoloration or seed coat mottling.
summary.quote: Symptoms of BPMV are green to yellow mottling (blotchiness) of younger leaves in the upper canopy. In severe cases leaves may show puckering and distortion and plants can be stunted. Pods can be misshapen with mottling and reduced in size. Infected plants may have green stems after the pods have matured. Seed from infected plants may appear smoky in color or have reduced size and quality due to seed coat mottling which will be brown or black depending on hilum color.

canonical.summary: CMV symptoms include stunting, mosaic patterns of light and dark green (or yellow and green) on leaves, malformation of leaves or growing points, yellow streaking or spotting on leaves, ring-spots or line patterns on leaves or fruit, flower color breaking, and distinct yellowing only of veins.
diagnostic_features: In tomato, the most characteristic symptom is filiformity (thin, shoestring-like leaves); in celery, downward curling of young petioles giving a flattened look; in cucurbits, severe stunting with prominent yellow foliar mosaic and greenish petals on flowers; in pepper, mosaic with leaves becoming dull green, brittle, and leathery, plus wrinkled, bumpy fruit.

summary.value: CMV symptoms include stunting, mosaic patterns of light and dark green (or yellow and green) on leaves, malformation of leaves or growing points, yellow streaking or spotting on leaves, ring-spots or line patterns on leaves or fruit, flower color breaking, and distinct yellowing only of veins.
summary.quote: Symptoms associated with CMV infections: stunting mosaic pattern of light and dark green (or yellow and green) on the leaves. malformation of leaves or growing points yellow streaking of leaves (especially monocots) yellow spotting on leaves ring-spots or line patterns on leaves or fruit flower color breaking distinct yellowing only of veins

diagnostic_features.value: In tomato, the most characteristic symptom is filiformity (thin, shoestring-like leaves); in celery, downward curling of young petioles giving a flattened look; in cucurbits, severe stunting with prominent yellow foliar mosaic and greenish petals on flowers; in pepper, mosaic with leaves becoming dull green, brittle, and leathery, plus wrinkled, bumpy fruit.
diagnostic_features.quote: The most characteristic symptom of CMV in tomato is filiformity, or the development of thin, shoestring-like leaves... The most easily identified symptom of CMV in celery is a downward curling of young petioles, which gives plants and opened or flattened look... Symptoms of CMV in cucurbits generally manifest as severe stunting, prominent yellow foliar mosaic, plant and leaf malformation... Flowers develop abnormalities and have greenish petals... leaves will become dull green, brittle, and leathery... Fruit has a wrinkled, bumpy appearance

look_alikes.value: tobacco mosaic virus; high temperature damage; insect feeding; growth regulator damage; herbicide damage; mineral deficiencies; mineral excesses
look_alikes.quote: Some of the above symptoms can also be caused by high temperature, insect feeding, growth regulators, herbicides, mineral deficiencies, and mineral excesses.

affected_parts.quote: leaves prominently curl downward, develop a mosaic pattern, and remain small. Flowers develop abnormalities and have greenish petals... Infected fruit are often distorted and discolored... plants appear stunted due to shortened internodes.

type_of_disease.quote: CMV is made up of nucleic acid (ribonucleic acid, RNA) surrounded by a protein coat.

canonical.summary: At the seedling stage, brown to dark spots appear on cotyledons; at the unifoliate (VC) stage, circular to oblong reddish-brown lesions that turn dark brown to black may be observed; mid to late season symptoms include light brown to grey superficial lesions in the stem, with microsclerotia in the vascular tissue, pith, and mature dry pods, giving cross-sections of the lower stem and taproot a marbled gray appearance.
diagnostic_features: A characteristic sign is the presence of microsclerotia in the vascular tissue, pith, and mature dry pods, with cross-sections of the lower stem and taproot streaked with gray hyphae and microsclerotia giving a marbled appearance; small black specks beneath the epidermis give a charcoal-sprinkled appearance.

canonical.summary: Symptoms begin on leaves in the lower canopy as small yellow, irregularly shaped spots that enlarge into gray-green, tan to dark reddish-brown angular lesions (2-5 mm); pustules (blisters) form within the lesions and break open to release masses of tan urediniospores, with heavy infection causing leaf yellowing, premature defoliation, and early maturity.

canonical.summary: Leaves develop large dark brown blotches with a green-gray edge, not confined by major leaf veins; stems show firm dark brown lesions; tomato fruit develop firm, dark brown, circular spots that may turn brown and remain firm; under humid conditions a thin powdery white fungal/mycelial growth (sporulation) appears on leaves, stems, and fruit; potato tubers become discolored, shrunken, and soft.

canonical.summary: Small black spots (⅛ to ¼ inch) on leaves often more prominent on undersides, sometimes with yellow halos; very small, pinpoint-like spots on fruit ranging from brown to black, which can be raised, flat or sunken, with darker green halos on green fruits and subtle yellow halos on ripe fruits.

summary.quote: Leaf symptoms of bacterial speck consist of small black spots (approximately ⅛ to ¼ inch in diameter) that often are more prominent on the undersides of leaves.   As the spots age, a yellow halo may develop around the edge.  Spots on fruit are very small (almost pinpoint-like) and do not penetrate very deeply into the tissue.   The spots can be raised, flat or sunken, and range in color from brown to black.  On unripe, green fruits, the spots often have darker green haloes, while on ripe fruits the spots can have subtle, yellow haloes.

diagnostic_features.quote: On large-leafed plants, only the tissue on one side of the mid-vein may wilt. ... Usually, the main stem of the affected plants remains upright even though all the leaves may wilt and die. Internal symptoms include light tan to yellow-brown discoloration of the vascular tissue. Long sections of infected stems reveal dark brown to black streaking in the vascular tissue as the disease progresses. As invasion proceeds, the pith and cortex of the stem become dark brown. ... Slimy, sticky ooze forms tan-white to brownish beads where the vascular tissue is cut. ... If one of the cut ends is suspended in a clear container of clean water, bacterial ooze will form a thread in the water. ... while Xcp can cause leaf spotting, Ralstonia does not.

type_of_disease.quote: Bacteria called Ralstonia solanacearum attack almost 200 plant species in 33 different plant families.

canonical.summary: On tomatoes, uppermost leaves appear bronzed with small brown lesions; on peppers, foliage develops small brown necrotic spots and/or chlorotic ring spots. Heavily infected leaves brown, roll, and wilt; fruit show yellow ringspots and brown necrotic sunken areas, and infected seedlings form a "shepherd's crook" and die.
diagnostic_features: Lesions caused by TSWV have blurred margins (whereas other diseases produce lesions with definite margins); infected seedlings form a distinctive "shepherd's crook" appearance; bronzing of upper sides of young leaves with later necrotic spots and downward leaf cupping; on ripe fruit, chlorotic spots and blotches with concentric rings, and on green fruit, slightly raised areas with faint concentric zones.

summary.value: On tomatoes, uppermost leaves appear bronzed with small brown lesions; on peppers, foliage develops small brown necrotic spots and/or chlorotic ring spots. Heavily infected leaves brown, roll, and wilt; fruit show yellow ringspots and brown necrotic sunken areas, and infected seedlings form a "shepherd's crook" and die.
summary.quote: On tomatoes infected with TSWV, the uppermost leaves appear bronzed with small brown lesions (Figure 1). Infected peppers form small brown necrotic spots and/or chlorotic ring spots on the foliage (Figure 2). Infected foliage on both tomatoes and peppers may become covered with so many spots that the entire leaf browns, rolls, and wilts. Infected seedlings form a "shepherd's crook" appearance and die (Figure 3). ... Symptoms on the fruit of both tomatoes and peppers typically include yellow ringspots and brown necrotic sunken areas

diagnostic_features.value: Lesions caused by TSWV have blurred margins (whereas other diseases produce lesions with definite margins); infected seedlings form a distinctive "shepherd's crook" appearance; bronzing of upper sides of young leaves with later necrotic spots and downward leaf cupping; on ripe fruit, chlorotic spots and blotches with concentric rings, and on green fruit, slightly raised areas with faint concentric zones.
diagnostic_features.quote: lesions caused by TSWV have blurred margins, while the margins of lesions caused by other diseases are definite.

look_alikes.value: early blight; other leaf spot diseases; other plant viruses
look_alikes.quote: Symptoms of TSWV may also resemble symptoms of early blight or other leaf spot diseases; however, lesions caused by TSWV have blurred margins, while the margins of lesions caused by other diseases are definite.

affected_parts.quote: On tomatoes infected with TSWV, the uppermost leaves appear bronzed with small brown lesions ... Infected seedlings form a "shepherd's crook" appearance and die ... Established plants that become infected are stunted with decreased yields.

pathogen_scientific_name.quote: Tomato spotted wilt virus in the tospovirus group

type_of_disease.quote: TSWV is a member of the genus, Orthotospovirus— a group of plant viruses vectored by arthropods, usually thrips.

canonical.summary: Circular, tan-to-gray spots (2-5 mm / <1/8 in) with darker brown margins and a yellow halo, dotted with dark raised pycnidia in the center; appear first on lower leaves and progress upward, causing leaves to yellow, brown, wither and drop. Stem lesions appear similar but darker; fruit lesions are uncommon.
diagnostic_features: Small dark raised pycnidia (fungal fruiting bodies) visible in the center of circular tan-to-gray lesions with dark-brown perimeter and yellow halo; on leaf undersides spots may appear darker green or water-soaked.

summary.value: Circular, tan-to-gray spots (2-5 mm / <1/8 in) with darker brown margins and a yellow halo, dotted with dark raised pycnidia in the center; appear first on lower leaves and progress upward, causing leaves to yellow, brown, wither and drop. summary.quote: Symptoms appear on the leaves as circular, tan-to-gray spots with darker brown margins and dotted with dark, raised pycnidia inside the lesion. canonical.summary: Early season symptoms include seed rot and pre- and postemergence damping off; the most characteristic symptom is a dark brown lesion on the lower stem that extends up from the taproot, often reaching several nodes and girdling the stem, stunting or killing the plant. In wet, waterlogged, compacted soils, infected stems appear bruised and soft, secondary roots are rotted, leaves turn yellow and brown, and plants wilt and die with leaves remaining attached.
diagnostic_features: A dark brown / dark chocolate-brown lesion or discoloration on the lower stem that extends from below the soil line up from the taproot, often reaching several nodes and girdling the stem; wilted leaves turn yellow and remain attached to the plant; plants are often killed in patches or in sections of rows.

summary.value: Early season symptoms include seed rot and pre- and postemergence damping off; the most characteristic symptom is a dark brown lesion on the lower stem that extends up from the taproot, often reaching several nodes and girdling the stem, stunting or killing the plant. summary.quote: Early season symptoms include seed rot and pre- and postemergence damping off. The most characteristic symptom of Phytophthora root and stem rot is a dark brown lesion on the lower stem that extends up from the taproot of the plant. The lesion often reaches as high as several nodes and will girdle soybean stems, restricting flow of nutrients and water, and stunting or killing the plant.

diagnostic_features.value: A dark brown / dark chocolate-brown lesion or discoloration on the lower stem that extends from below the soil line up from the taproot, often reaching several nodes and girdling the stem; wilted leaves turn yellow and remain attached to the plant; plants are often killed in patches or in sections of rows.
diagnostic_features.quote: The most characteristic symptom of Phytophthora root and stem rot is a dark brown lesion on the lower stem that extends up from the taproot of the plant. The lesion often reaches as high as several nodes and will girdle soybean stems

look_alikes.quote: It is important to recognize that Pythium is a related pathogen that causes similar damage and symptoms on seeds and seedlings... This disease can be confused with stem canker, which usually starts at a node and spreads up and down the stems of infected plants.

As P. sojae colonizes the plant, the vascular system and lower stem tissues can occur on the lower stems.

canonical.summary: Infections begin as small, angular, water-soaked spots that turn yellow and then brown as the tissue dies, surrounded by yellowish-green halos; spots often merge to form large dead patches on leaves, and dead tissue may fall out giving leaves a ragged appearance. Pods develop small water-soaked lesions that turn necrotic, and infected seeds may be discolored with a slimy bacterial film.
diagnostic_features: Brown, angular lesions with chlorotic/yellowish-green halos around water-soaked tissue, most common on young leaves in the mid to upper canopy; bacterial streaming visible as a dark cloud of bacterial cells when a cross section of a lesion is submerged in water and observed under microscope; absence of fungal structures distinguishes it from Septoria brown spot.

summary.value: Infections begin as small, angular, water-soaked spots that turn yellow and then brown as the tissue dies, surrounded by yellowish-green halos; spots often merge to form large dead patches on leaves, and dead tissue may fall out giving leaves a ragged appearance. summary.quote: Infections begin as small, angular, water-soaked spots that turn yellow and then brown as the tissue dies. The spots darken and are surrounded by yellowish-green halos. diagnostic_features.value: Brown, angular lesions with chlorotic/yellowish-green halos around water-soaked tissue, most common on young leaves in the mid to upper canopy; bacterial streaming visible as a dark cloud of bacterial cells when a cross section of a lesion is submerged in water and observed under microscope; absence of fungal structures distinguishes it from Septoria brown spot.
diagnostic_features.quote: The most common symptoms on leaves are brown, angular lesions with chlorotic halos... As a common diagnostic test, a cross section of a lesion is submerged in water and observed under the microscope. The dark cloud emerging from the leaf tissue contains millions of bacterial cells, commonly referred to as bacterial streaming.

canonical.summary: Diseased plants first appear wilted with grayish-green leaves that later turn brown to tan; lesions develop first at stem nodes during or after flowering, the tops wilt and die, infected stems become soft, watery, and covered with white fluffy moldy growth in moist conditions, and dry dead stems develop a bleached, white appearance.
diagnostic_features: Hard, black, irregularly shaped sclerotia (with pink to white centers) that look similar to rodent droppings develop on or inside infected stems and pods; white fluffy mycelia cover grayish to bleached white stem lesions in moist conditions, and infected seeds may be flat and shriveled or replaced by sclerotia.

summary.value: Diseased plants first appear wilted with grayish-green leaves that later turn brown to tan; lesions develop first at stem nodes during or after flowering, the tops wilt and die, infected stems become soft, watery, and covered with white fluffy moldy growth in moist conditions, and dry dead stems develop a bleached, white appearance.
summary.quote: Diseased plants first appear wilted and with grayish green leaves that later turn brown to tan (Figure 2). The stems also have grayish to bleached white lesions that are covered by white fluffy mycelia under conditions of high moisture (Figures 3A and 3B). In later stages, sclerotia can be observed inside or outside of the stems and pods (Figures 3C and 3D).

diagnostic_features.value: Hard, black, irregularly shaped sclerotia (with pink to white centers) that look similar to rodent droppings develop on or inside infected stems and pods; white fluffy mycelia cover grayish to bleached white stem lesions in moist conditions, and infected seeds may be flat and shriveled or replaced by sclerotia.
diagnostic_features.quote: This fungus produces a survival structure called a sclerotium (pl. sclerotia), which is black, hard and irregular in shape with a pink to white center (Figure 1A). ... The stems also have grayish to bleached white lesions that are covered by white fluffy mycelia under conditions of high moisture (Figures 3A and 3B). In later stages, sclerotia can be observed inside or outside of the stems and pods (Figures 3C and 3D). If the seeds were infected during the early stages of development, they may have a flat and shriveled appearance, or may be replaced by sclerotia.

canonical.summary: Symptoms begin as small, light green spots (without water-soaking) on leaves with raised centers; light-colored pustules develop in the center of lesions on both upper and lower leaf surfaces; lesions can coalesce into large irregular dead patches and small raised spots may develop on pods. As spots mature, lesions and pustules turn brown.
diagnostic_features: Bacterial pustule lesions normally lack an opening and do not produce spores (unlike soybean rust pustules which have a small opening for spore release); if an opening is present, it is typically a linear crack across the surface of the pustule. Water soaking will not be present, and lesions will have raised centers (unlike bacterial blight). Magnification of 20X or higher is recommended for diagnosis.

canonical.summary: Botrytis produces a dense velvety gray brown spore mass on infected tissue which when disturbed releases a cloud of spores; tomato leaf and stem lesions are tan to brown, stem lesions may expand in concentric rings to girdle the entire stem causing wilting above the infection site, and fruit lesions are typically a whitish soft rot.
diagnostic_features: Dense velvety gray brown spore mass that releases a cloud of spores when disturbed; stem lesions expanding in concentric rings to girdle the stem; whitish soft rot on fruit with skin rupturing near the center of the decayed area; small 3-8 mm whitish rings on the fruit surface known as ghost spots; under a hand lens spore-bearing structures resemble bunches of grapes.

lesion_morphology.quote: Leaves are uniformly tan-brown and crisped, and a single fruit is collapsed and dark brown without any visible white cottony tuft or sporulating crown.

affected_organs.quote: Foliage is fully wilted and desiccated, the stem and side branches show dry necrotic tissue, and one fruit on an upper truss is collapsed and brown.

spread_pattern.quote: Symptoms cover the entire excavated plant uniformly from base to tip rather than being confined to a few fruit or a lower-canopy zone.

variations_from_canonical.value: Canonical describes water-soaked fruit lesions covered with a thin cotton-like white mycelial tuft and dark sporulation crowning it — none of those features is visible in this image.; No coarse fungal hyphal strands or 'nests of mold' linking adjacent fruit are visible; only one shriveled fruit is shown.; Image presents as a whole-plant collapse with desiccated foliage and stem, whereas the canonical reference focuses on fruit/root rot morphology rather than systemic foliar wilt.; The plant has been uprooted and posed on a cloth with a ruler, so the field spread pattern cannot be observed and is inferred only from the specimen.

canonical.summary: White mold appears at flowering with water-soaked areas on flowers and at stem joints; stems are quickly killed and dry to a bleached appearance, with white cottony mycelium and large irregular black sclerotia, and infected fruits turn gray and rot.
diagnostic_features: White, cottony mycelium that soon produces large, irregularly shaped, black sclerotia; bleached appearance of dried stems; sclerotia on infected fruits usually produced at the point of attachment with the plant.

summary.value: White mold appears at flowering with water-soaked areas on flowers and at stem joints; stems are quickly killed and dry to a bleached appearance, with white cottony mycelium and large irregular black sclerotia, and infected fruits turn gray and rot.
summary.quote: White mold generally appears on tomato plants at flowering. Symptoms include water-soaked areas on flowers and at stem joints where senescent flower petals have fallen. The infection quickly kills stems, which eventually dry and take on a bleached appearance. ... Affected areas generally show white, cottony mycelium that soon produces large, irregularly shaped, black sclerotia . Infected fruits turn gray and rot.

Maine.lesion_morphology: A girdling stem lesion at a branch junction with bleached light-tan, frayed/shredded outer tissue on one side and a discrete pad of dense white cottony mycelium on the green side, with no clearly visible black sclerotia.
variations_from_canonical: Image is a studio/laboratory photograph of an excised stem on a gray background, not an in-field flowering plant.

The bleached stem tissue is markedly frayed and shredded into fibrous strands, more disintegrated than the canonical 'dry, bleached' description implies.
